title: List decoding with double samplers
speaker: Inbal Livni-Navon
abstract: The ABNNR encoding is a classical encoding scheme that amplifies the distance of an error correcting code. The encoding takes an error correcting code with a small distance and constructs an error correcting code with distance approaching one, by using a bipartite expander graph.   The ABNNR encoding has an efficient decoding algorithm, which can correct a codeword with not too many corrupted coordinates. However, if half or more of the coordinates are corrupted, the decoding algorithm does not work.

proof_stages.label: List-decoding target for ABNNR amplification
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets the goal: starting from a constant-distance error-correcting code C with only ordinary decoding up to δ/2, list decode its ABNNR distance-amplified code C′ up to radius approaching one.

proof_stages.label: ABNNR neighbor-bundling encoding and distance amplification
proof_stages.summary: The ABNNR encoding is defined using a constant-degree bipartite graph from coordinates V0 to positions V1, with each V1-position storing its neighbors' bits as one alphabet symbol; distance is amplified when every δ-sized discrepancy set in V0 has neighbors covering almost all of V1.

proof_stages.label: Sampler condition for majority unique decoding
proof_stages.summary: A sampler graph is introduced and used to justify the standard majority decoder: fewer than half corrupted V1-symbols cannot force wrong majorities on more than δ/2 of V0, so the base decoder recovers the original word.

proof_stages.label: Double-sampler strengthening
proof_stages.summary: Because majority decoding breaks down near half the positions while list decoding must work near distance one, the graph is required to sit inside a three-layer inclusion structure V0,V1,V2 with global sampling between adjacent layers and local sampling inside every T in V2.

proof_stages.label: Construction barriers and high-dimensional-expander source
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that natural random and expander-walk attempts do not satisfy the local requirements, and that the known way to obtain the needed linear-size constant-degree double samplers is to take appropriate layers/faces of high-dimensional expanders.

proof_stages.label: Main theorem and local list formation
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem asserts a polynomial-time list-decoding algorithm for ABNNR encodings built from double samplers, and the algorithm begins by enumerating for each constant-size T in V2 a bounded local list of candidate restrictions x|T whose local encoding is close to the received word.

proof_stages.label: Consistency matchings and unique-games instance
proof_stages.summary: The local lists are patched by matching candidates on intersecting T's, with a dynamically chosen separation radius ensuring uniqueness, thereby producing a unique-constraint graph whose vertices are the V2-sets and whose alphabets are the corresponding local lists.

proof_stages.label: Expander unique-games solution and reconstruction
proof_stages.summary: Since the V1–V2 sampler supplies an expander and the true codeword induces a high-value assignment, an expander unique-games solver is used as a black box to recover globally consistent local choices, reconstruct an x close to the target, and output the corresponding encoded codeword/list.

examples.name: Two-neighbor bit-bundling toy coordinate
examples.note: A small displayed word with vertices reading symbols such as 01 and 10 makes concrete that each right vertex outputs one larger-alphabet letter formed from its neighbors' bits.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the encoding on a toy two-neighbor coordinate, bundling neighbor bits into letters such as 01 and 10

passes.reason: It is just an illustrative ordinary bundled coordinate of the ABNNR encoding.

examples.name: Binary/direct-output variant
examples.note: In response to a rate/alphabet question, the speaker contrasts bundling neighbor bits into one symbol with outputting them as binary bits, illustrating the alphabet-size versus near-one-distance tradeoff.
examples.engagement_reason: the binary/direct variant is described as an alternative and later related work, but no construction or decoding is worked through on it

passes.reason: The binary/direct-output variant is used to show that avoiding alphabet bundling loses the near-one distance property.

examples.name: Half-density subset in a sampler
examples.note: Taking a subset of half of V0 illustrates the sampler condition by saying that almost every right vertex should see about half of its incident edges inside that subset.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker instantiates the sampler definition on a half-size subset and explains via the indicator function why typical vertices see half their edges there

examples.name: Random-walk subsets on an expander
examples.note: Using long and short random walks on an expander has good global features but fails the required local sampling because local pieces can see mostly one side, clarifying why the double-sampler condition is stronger.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the random-walk-on-expander construction and checks a local obstruction using a zero-one function on the walk

examples.name: One-coordinate difference without the base-code promise
examples.note: The speaker notes that if x is not required to lie in the base code, two strings differing in one coordinate may have encodings differing in only constantly many positions, which explains why the no-promise version can only yield an approximate-list statement.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker examines two strings differing in one coordinate without the base-code promise and derives that their encodings differ only on constantly many positions

passes.reason: A one-coordinate difference is the minimum nonzero Hamming-distance case and is used to show failure without the base-code promise.
A pair differing in one coordinate is the minimal-distance case and is used to show the encoding is not a genuine code without the base-code promise.
A singleton coordinate difference is the minimal nonzero Hamming-distance case and is used to show the encoding is not a genuine code without the base-code promise.
A one-coordinate difference is the minimum nonzero Hamming-separation case and exposes the collapsed distance without the base-code promise.

a pair at Hamming distance one is the minimal nonzero-distance case and obstructs list decoding without the code promise
A pair differing in one coordinate is the minimum nonzero Hamming-distance case and is used to show the encoding is not a code without the base-code promise.
Two ambient strings differing in one coordinate form the minimum-distance obstruction when the base-code promise is removed.

Two strings differing in one coordinate give the minimum nonzero Hamming separation and show failure without the base-code promise.

A pair differing in one coordinate has minimum positive Hamming distance and is used to show the no-base-code-promise encoding is not genuinely list-decodable.
